children.name: Emberiza
children.description: Seed-eating birds of open habitats. The Yellowhammer's lazy summer song is the defining sound of Swedish farmland hedgerows; it is now declining rapidly across Europe due to agricultural intensification. The Ortolan Bunting has collapsed in Sweden and is the subject of urgent conservation measures.

children.name: Emberiza citrinella
children.description: The yellow-headed gulsparv, whose wheezy 'a-little-bit-of-bread-and-no-cheese' song epitomises Swedish farmland summers. Now declining sharply across Europe with the intensification of agriculture.

children.name: Emberiza schoeniclus
children.description: A reed-bed bunting, the male unmistakeable with a black head and white collar, singing a simple jangling phrase from a swaying stem. It is widespread in wetlands throughout Sweden.

children.name: Emberiza hortulana
children.description: A subtly beautiful bunting of open farmland with a pink bill and pale eye-ring, once widespread but now collapsed in Sweden and the focus of urgent conservation. It is still controversially trapped for food in parts of France.

children.name: Emberiza pusilla
children.description: A tiny, finely streaked bunting with a chestnut face, breeding in the birch and willow scrub of the far north. It winters in southern Asia and is a scarce passage migrant in much of Sweden.

children.name: Emberiza calandra
children.description: A dumpy, streaky bunting of open farmland whose jangling song is likened to rattling keys; has declined steeply with agricultural intensification.

children.name: Emberiza cirlus
children.description: A southern relative of the yellowhammer with a black throat; in Britain it became a flagship for farmland conservation after a dramatic recovery in Devon.

children.name: Emberiza cia
children.description: A grey-headed bunting of warm rocky hillsides and vineyards across southern Europe and Asia, with bold black face stripes.

children.name: Emberiza rustica
children.description: A chestnut-and-white bunting of northern bogs and wet forest, breeding across the taiga; globally Vulnerable after a steep decline driven by trapping in East Asia.

children.name: Emberiza aureola
children.description: Once one of Eurasia's most abundant birds, this golden bunting has collapsed catastrophically from mass trapping in China and is now Critically Endangered.

children.name: Emberiza leucocephalos
children.description: The eastern counterpart of the yellowhammer, the male with a white-and-chestnut head; breeds in Siberian pine forest and hybridises where ranges meet.

children.name: Emberiza melanocephala
children.description: A large, bright yellow bunting with a black hood, breeding in southeastern Europe and wintering in India.

children.name: Emberiza bruniceps
children.description: A Central Asian bunting with a rufous head and yellow underparts, breeding in scrub and farmland and migrating to the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Emberiza caesia
children.description: A close relative of the ortolan with a blue-grey head and rusty throat, breeding on dry Mediterranean and Middle Eastern hillsides.

children.name: Emberiza chrysophrys
children.description: A neat Siberian bunting with a bright yellow eyebrow, breeding in central Siberian forest and wintering in southern China; a rare vagrant to Europe.

children.name: Emberiza elegans
children.description: An East Asian bunting with a short crest and yellow throat, breeding in forest of China, Korea, and the Russian Far East.

children.name: Emberiza spodocephala
children.description: A common East Asian bunting of damp scrub, the male with a greenish-grey hood; winters abundantly across southern China and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Emberiza fucata
children.description: A bunting of Asian grasslands and wet meadows, named for its chestnut ear-coverts; breeds from the Himalayas to Japan.

children.name: Emberiza cioides
children.description: A widespread East Asian bunting of dry scrubby hillsides, with a boldly striped chestnut-and-white head.

children.name: Emberiza buchanani
children.description: A pale desert-edge bunting of Central Asian rocky slopes, wintering in India.

children.name: Emberiza stewarti
children.description: A bunting of dry Himalayan foothills, the male with a grey crown and black mask; breeds in Central Asia and winters in northern India.

children.name: Emberiza striolata
children.description: A tame, sparrow-like bunting of arid North Africa and the Middle East that readily nests in and around buildings in desert towns.

children.name: Emberiza tahapisi
children.description: A small rufous bunting with a striped head, widespread on rocky African hillsides and into Arabia.

children.name: Emberiza flaviventris
children.description: A bright golden-and-chestnut bunting of African savanna and open woodland, common across much of sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Emberiza capensis
children.description: A grey-and-rufous bunting of rocky slopes in southern Africa, often tame around campsites and mountain huts.

children.name: Emberiza sulphurata
children.description: A scarce bunting that breeds only in Japan and winters in the Philippines and southern China; classed as Vulnerable due to its small range.

children.name: Emberiza variabilis
children.description: A skulking dark bunting of dense forest undergrowth in Japan and the Russian Far East, the male slaty-grey all over.

children.name: Emberiza koslowi
children.description: A high-altitude bunting endemic to the eastern Tibetan Plateau, with a striking chestnut-and-black pattern; Near Threatened.

children.name: Emberiza godlewskii
children.description: A rock bunting relative of Central Asian and Chinese mountains, with grey head and chestnut crown stripes; sings from boulders on arid slopes.

children.name: Emberiza affinis
children.description: A small bunting of West African savanna and woodland edge from Senegal to western Ethiopia. The male has a chestnut-brown rump and streaked underparts. Forages on the ground in mixed flocks and is often overlooked among other small finches.

children.name: Emberiza cineracea
children.description: A scarce and localised bunting breeding on dry rocky hillsides in southwestern Turkey and a few Greek islands, wintering in Sudan and Ethiopia. The male is largely grey with a yellow-tinged head. Listed as Vulnerable due to its small and declining population.

children.name: Emberiza forbesi
children.description: A poorly known bunting of rocky inselbergs and hill scrub in a narrow band across West Africa from Nigeria to Ethiopia. Named after naturalist and explorer Henry Ogg Forbes, who collected specimens in Africa and Asia.

children.name: Emberiza impetuani
children.description: A small, pale, streaked bunting of arid and semi-arid southern Africa, superficially resembling a lark in its sandy tones and terrestrial habits. Found from Namibia and Botswana to South Africa, usually in groups on stony desert plains.

children.name: Emberiza jankowskii
children.description: One of the world's most threatened songbirds, Critically Endangered and possibly at risk of imminent extinction. Breeds in a tiny area of open steppe in northeastern China; its population has collapsed due to agricultural expansion and overgrazing. Named after Polish naturalist Michael Jankowski.

children.name: Emberiza pallasi
children.description: A reed bunting of Central and East Asian reed beds, rivers, and wet scrub, wintering in China and Southeast Asia. Smaller and paler than the common reed bunting, with a smaller bill. Named after 18th-century German naturalist Peter Simon Pallas.

children.name: Emberiza poliopleura
children.description: A dry-country bunting of the Horn of Africa, found in Somalia, Ethiopia, and adjacent Kenya in thornbush and arid scrub. The male has a pale grey head and rufous flanks. A little-studied species largely confined to the poorly surveyed Greater Horn region.

children.name: Emberiza socotrana
children.description: An island endemic entirely restricted to Socotra in the Arabian Sea, one of the world's most biodiverse archipelagos. Inhabits rocky plateau and semi-arid montane scrub. Classified as Vulnerable due to its tiny range and sensitivity to habitat change.

children.name: Emberiza tristrami
children.description: A neat East Asian bunting with a boldly striped black-and-white head, breeding in mixed forest of Manchuria and Siberia and wintering in southern China and Southeast Asia. Named after English clergyman and ornithologist Henry Baker Tristram.

children.name: Emberiza yessoensis
children.description: A reed bunting of wetlands and marshes in Japan and coastal northeastern China, with a restricted wintering range in coastal East Asia. Listed as Vulnerable; its population has declined sharply due to loss of reed bed and coastal marsh habitat.

children.name: Plectrophenax
children.description: Hardy buntings of Arctic coasts and tundra, the males strikingly white in summer.

children.name: Plectrophenax nivalis
children.description: A hardy bunting of the high mountains and Arctic coasts, the males flashing brilliant white in their wings as winter flocks swirl over snowy shores. It breeds on Sweden's highest fells.

children.name: Plectrophenax hyperboreus
children.description: A nearly all-white bunting breeding on just a few remote Bering Sea islands off Alaska; one of North America's most range-restricted songbirds.

children.name: Calcarius
children.description: Ground-dwelling buntings of open tundra, the males with chestnut and black head patterns.

children.name: Calcarius lapponicus
children.description: A ground-loving bunting of the open tundra and mountain heath, the breeding male sporting a black face and chestnut nape. Sweden's mountain birch and willow zone holds its breeding population.

children.name: Calcarius ornatus
children.description: A longspur of the mixed-grass and shortgrass prairies of the North American Great Plains, the male with a chestnut nape and black-and-white underparts. Breeds from Alberta to Minnesota; winters in the southern Great Plains and northern Mexico. Declining with loss of native grassland.

children.name: Calcarius pictus
children.description: A buff-and-black longspur of subarctic tundra from Alaska to Hudson Bay, wintering in prairie grasslands of the central United States. Unusually polygamous among songbirds; forms small colonies where females may mate with multiple males in a single season.

children.name: Rhynchophanes
children.description: Monotypic genus containing the thick-billed longspur of North American shortgrass prairies. Distinguished from true Calcarius longspurs by a notably heavier bill and more terrestrial habits. Was formerly placed in Calcarius but molecular studies support generic separation.

children.name: Rhynchophanes mccownii
children.description: A stocky longspur of North American shortgrass prairie, formerly called McCown's Longspur. Breeds in Montana, Wyoming, and the Dakotas; winters in the southern Great Plains. The chestnut wing-patches and white tail patches are distinctive in flight. Named after John Porter McCown, who collected the type specimen.
